response.answer:
A shear bolt check verifies that a connection can safely transfer applied shear forces without the bolt failing in shear or the connected plates failing in bearing. Per clause 3.6.1 and Table 3.4, the design shear resistance per shear plane is calculated as **$F_{v,Rd} = \frac{\alpha_v f_{ub} A}{\gamma_{M2}}$**, while bearing resistance is determined using the formula in Table 8.4. This process ensures the connection meets the requirements for Category A bearing-type connections as defined in clause 3.4.1. This follows EN 1993-1-3, Cl. Table 8.4 (Design resistances for bolts) with supporting guidance from EN 1993-1-8, Cl. Table 3.4 (Design resistance for individual fasteners subjected to shear and/or tension). --- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-3, Table 8.4 — Design resistances for bolts

Bolts loaded in shear:

Bearing resistance:

$$F_{\text{b,Rd}} = 2.5 \alpha_b k_t f_u d t / \gamma_{M2}$$ with $\alpha_b$ is the smallest of 1,0 or $e_1$ / (3d) and $k_t = (0.8 t + 1.5) / 2.5$ for 0,75 mm ≤ t ≤ 1,25 mm; $k_t = 1.0$ for t > 1,25 mm

Net-section resistance:

$$F_{\text{n,Rd}} = (1 + 3 r (d_o / u - 0.3)) A_{\text{net}} f_u / \gamma_{M2}$$ but $F_{\text{n,Rd}} \leq A_{\text{net}} f_u / \gamma_{M2}$ with:

$$r = [\text{number of bolts at the cross-section}] / [\text{total number of bolts in the connection}]$$

$$u = 2 e_2$$ but $u \leq p_2$

Shear resistance:

- for strength grades 4.6, 5.6 and 8.8:

$$F_{\text{v,Rd}} = 0.6 f_{\text{ub}} A_s / \gamma_{M2}$$

- for strength grades 4.8, 5.8, 6.8 and 10.9:

$$F_{\text{v,Rd}} = 0.5 f_{\text{ub}} A_s / \gamma_{M2}$$

Conditions:

$$F_{\text{v,Rd}} \geq 1.2 \Sigma F_{\text{b,Rd}} \text{ or } \Sigma F_{\text{v,Rd}} \geq 1.2 F_{\text{n,Rd}}$$

Bolts loaded in tension:

Pull-through resistance: Pull-through resistance $F_{\text{p,Rd}}$ to be determined by testing $^{*4}$.

Pull-out resistance: Not relevant for bolts.

Tension resistance: $F_{\text{t,Rd}} = 0.9 f_{\text{ub}} A_s / \gamma_{M2}$

Conditions:

$$F_{\text{t,Rd}} \geq \Sigma F_{\text{p,Rd}}$$

Range of validity:

$$e_1 \geq 1.0 d_o \quad p_1 \geq 3 d_o \quad 0.75 \text{ mm} \leq t < 3 \text{ mm}$$
Minimum bolt size: M6
$$e_2 \geq 1.5 d_o \quad p_2 \geq 3 d_o$$
Str …

2. EN 1993-1-8, Table 3.4 — Design resistance for individual fasteners subjected to shear and/or tension

| Failure mode | Bolts | Rivets |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Shear resistance per shear plane | $F_{v,Rd} = \frac{\alpha_v f_{ub} A}{\gamma_M 2}$ |
| - where the shear plane passes through the threaded portion of the bolt ($A$ is the tensile stress area of the bolt $A_s$): |
| - for classes 4.6, 5.6 and 8.8: $\alpha_v = 0, 6$ |
| - for classes 4.8, 5.8, 6.8 and 10.9: $\alpha_v = 0, 5$ |
| - where the shear plane passes through the unthreaded portion of the bolt ($A$ is the gross cross section of the bolt): $\alpha_v = 0, 6$ | $F_{v,Rd} = \frac{0,6 f_{ur} A_0}{\gamma_M 2}$ |

Bearing resistance 1), 2), 3)

$$F_{b,Rd} = \frac{k_1 \alpha_b f_u dt}{\gamma_M 2}$$

where $\alpha_b$ is the smallest of $\alpha_d$; $\frac{f_{ub}}{f_u}$ or 1,0;

in the direction of load transfer:

- for end bolts: $\alpha_d = \frac{e_1}{3d_0}$; for inner bolts: $\alpha_d = \frac{p_1}{3d_0} - \frac{1}{4}$ perpendicular to the direction of load transfer:

$$F_{ac2} -$$ for edge bolts: $k_1$ is the smallest of $2,8 \frac{e_2}{d_0} - 1,7,1,4 \frac{p_2}{d_0} - 1,7$ and $2,5 \frac{p_2}{d_0} - 1,7$ or 2,5

- for inner bolts: $k_1$ is the smallest of $1,4 \frac{p_2}{d_0} - 1,7$ or 2,5

Tension resistance 2)

$$F_{t,Rd} = \frac{k_2 f_{ub} A_s}{\gamma_M 2}$$

where $k_2 = 0,63$ for countersunk bolt, otherwise $k_2 = 0,9$.

Punching shear resistance

$$B_{p,Rd} = 0,6 \pi d_m t_p f_u / \gamma_M 2$$

No check needed

C …

3. EN 1993-1-8, Cl. 3.6.1 — Bolts and rivets

(1) The design resistance for an individual fastener subjected to shear and/or tension is given in Table 3.4.

(2) For preloaded bolts in accordance with 3.1.2(1) the design preload, $F_{p,Cd}$, to be used in design calculations should be taken as:

$$F_{p,Cd} = 0.7 f_{ub} A_s / \gamma_{M7}$$

... (3.1)

NOTE: Where the preload is not used in design calculations see note to Table 3.2.

(3) The design resistances for tension and for shear through the threaded portion of a bolt given in Table 3.4 should only be used for bolts manufactured in conformity with 1.2.4 Reference Standard: Group 4.

For bolts with cut threads, such as anchor bolts or tie rods fabricated from round steel bars where the threads comply with EN 1090, the relevant values from Table 3.4 should be used. For bolts with cut threads where the threads do not comply with EN 1090 the relevant values from Table 3.4 should be multiplied by a factor of 0.85.

(4) The design shear resistance $F_{v,Rd}$ given in Table 3.4 should only be used where the bolts are used in holes with nominal clearances not exceeding those for normal holes as specified in 1.2.7 Reference Standards: Group 7.

(5) M12 and M14 bolts may also be used in 2 mm clearance holes provided that the design resistance of the bolt group based on bearing $AC_2$ is less than or equal to $AC_2$ the design resistance of the bolt group based on bolt shear. In a …

4. EN 1993-1-8, Cl. 3.4.1 — Shear connections

(1) Bolted connections loaded in shear should be designed as one of the following:

a) Category A: Bearing type
In this category bolts from class 4.6 up to and including class 10.9 should be used. No preloading and special provisions for contact surfaces are required. The design ultimate shear load should not exceed the design shear resistance, obtained from 3.6, nor the design bearing resistance, obtained from 3.6 and 3.7.

b) Category B: Slip-resistant at serviceability limit state
In this category preloaded bolts in accordance with 3.1.2(1) should be used. Slip should not occur at the serviceability limit state. The design serviceability shear load should not exceed the design slip resistance, obtained from 3.9. The design ultimate shear load should not exceed the design shear resistance, obtained from 3.6, nor the design bearing resistance, obtained from 3.6 and 3.7.

c) Category C: Slip-resistant at ultimate limit state
In this category preloaded bolts in accordance with 3.1.2(1) should be used. Slip should not occur at the ultimate limit state. The design ultimate shear load should not exceed the design slip resistance, obtained from 3.9, nor the design bearing resistance, obtained from 3.6 and 3.7. In addition for a connection in tension, the design plastic resistance of the net cross-section at bolt holes $N_{\text{net,Rd}}$, (see 6.2 of EN 1993-1-1), should be checked, …

5. EN 1993-1-8, Cl. 3.9.2 — Combined tension and shear

(1) If a slip-resistant connection is subjected to an applied tensile force, $F_{t,Ed}$ or $F_{t,Ed,ser}$, in addition to the shear force, $F_{v,Ed}$ or $F_{v,Ed,ser}$, tending to produce slip, the design slip resistance per bolt should be taken as follows:

$$F_{s,Rd,ser} = \frac{k_s n \mu (F_{p,C} - 0.8 F_{t,Ed,ser})}{\gamma_{M3,ser}}$$ ... (3.8a)

for a category B connection:

$$F_{s,Rd} = \frac{k_s n \mu (F_{p,C} - 0.8 F_{t,Ed})}{\gamma_{M3}}$$ ... (3.8b)

(2) If, in a moment connection, a contact force on the compression side counterbalances the applied tensile force no reduction in slip resistance is required.

6. EN 1993-1-2, Table D.1 — Strength Reduction Factors for Bolts and Welds

| Temperature $\theta_a$ | Reduction factor for bolts, $k_{b,\theta}$ (Tension and shear) | Reduction factor for welds, $k_{w,\theta}$ (AC2) |

Cross-sections are classified into four classes based on their ability to develop plastic resistance and rotation capacity, as defined in EN 1993-1-4 clause 5.5.2. Compression elements are classified as Class 1, 2, or 3 according to limits in Table 5.2, while elements failing these criteria are designated as Class 4 per clause 5.2.2. The classification parameter $\varepsilon$ is determined by the formula $\varepsilon = 0.85 \left[ \frac{235}{f_y} \right]^{0.5}$. This follows EN 1993-1-4, Cl. 5.2.2 (Classification of compression elements) with supporting guidance from EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2 (Classification). --- **References:**

(1) Compression elements of cross-sections should be classified as Class 1, 2 or 3 depending upon the limits specified in Table 5.2. Those compression elements that do not meet the criteria for Class 3 should be classified as Class 4 elements.

2. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2 — Classification

(1) Four classes of cross-sections are defined, as follows:

- Class 1 cross-sections are those which can form a plastic hinge with the rotation capacity required from plastic analysis without reduction of the resistance.

- Class 2 cross-sections are those which can develop their plastic moment resistance, but have limited rotation capacity because of local buckling.

- Class 3 cross-sections are those in which the stress in the extreme compression fibre of the steel member assuming an elastic distribution of stresses can reach the yield strength, but local buckling is liable to prevent development of the plastic moment resistance.

- Class 4 cross-sections are those in which local buckling will occur before the attainment of yield stress in one or more parts of the cross-section.

response.answer:
The classification of I-sections is determined by the local buckling resistance of their constituent compression elements—specifically the flanges and the web—which dictates the section's ability to develop plastic or elastic resistance. Per EN 1993-1-4: 2006 (E) Clause 5.5.2(1), cross-sections are categorized into four classes based on their structural performance: * **Class 1**: Sections that can form a plastic hinge with the rotation capacity required from plastic analysis without reduction of the resistance. * **Class 2**: Sections that can develop their plastic moment resistance. * **Class 3**: Elements that meet the limits specified in Table 5.2 but do not qualify for Class 1 or 2. * **Class 4**: Elements that do not meet the criteria for Class 3 and are subject to local buckling. The classification process relies on the width-to-thickness ratios of the compression elements, which are governed by the parameter $\varepsilon$. According to Clause 4.2.2(1), this parameter is calculated as: $\varepsilon = 0.85 \left[ \frac{235}{f_y} \right]^{0.5}$ where $f_y$ is the yield strength at $20^{\circ}C$. There are specific provisions for mixed classification scenarios. For instance, under Clause 6.2.2.4(1), if an I-section has a Class 3 web and Class 1 or 2 flanges, it may be classified as an effective Class 2 cross-section. In this case, the portion of the web in compression is replaced by a part of $20\varepsilon t_w$ adjacent to the compression flange, with another part of $20\varepsilon t_w$ adjacent to the tension flange. Additionally, if the flange is subject to uniform compression and contains intermediate stiffeners, Clause 5.5.3.4.2(1) dictates that the effective cross-section consists of the reduced effective areas $A_{s,\text{red}}$, which includes two strips of width $0,5b_{\text{eff}}$ (or $15 t$) adjacent to the stiffener. This follows EN 1993-1-4, Cl. 5.2.2 (Classification of compression elements) with supporting guidance from EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2 (Classification). --- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-4, Cl. 5.2.2 — Classification of compression elements

(1) Compression elements of cross-sections should be classified as Class 1, 2 or 3 depending upon the limits specified in Table 5.2. Those compression elements that do not meet the criteria for Class 3 should be classified as Class 4 elements.

EN 1993-1-4: 2006 (E)

2. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2 — Classification

(1) Four classes of cross-sections are defined, as follows:

- Class 1 cross-sections are those which can form a plastic hinge with the rotation capacity required from plastic analysis without reduction of the resistance.

- Class 2 cross-sections are those which can develop their plastic moment resistance, but have limited rotation capacity because of local buckling.

- Class 3 cross-sections are those in which the stress in the extreme compression fibre of the steel member assuming an elastic distribution of stresses can reach the yield strength, but local buckling is liable to prevent development of the plastic moment resistance.

- Class 4 cross-sections are those in which local buckling will occur before the attainment of yield stress in one or more parts of the cross-section.

(2) In Class 4 cross sections effective widths may be used to make the necessary allowances for reductions in resistance due to the effects of local buckling, see EN 1993-1-5, 4.4 AC2.

(3) The classification of a cross-section depends on the width to thickness ratio of the parts subject to compression.

(4) Compression parts include every part of a cross-section which is either totally or partially in compression under the load combination considered.

(5) The various compression parts in a cross-section (such as a web or flange) can, in general, be in different classes.

response.answer:
The classification of cross-sections is a design procedure used to determine the local buckling resistance of individual elements, which dictates whether an I-section can achieve plastic or elastic resistance. According to Clause 5.5.2, there are four classes of cross-sections defined by their ability to resist local buckling: * **Class 1 cross-sections** are those which can form a plastic hinge with the rotation capacity required from plastic analysis without reduction of the resistance. * **Class 2 cross-sections** are those which can develop their plastic moment resistance. The classification process relies on limiting the width-to-thickness ratios of the compression elements within the section. Per Clause 5.2.1, for design by calculation, the overall width-to-thickness ratios $b/t$ and $h/t$ should not exceed **400**. While the provided evidence specifically details limits for angles and tubular sections in Table 5.2, the methodology for I-sections follows the same principle of restricting element proportions to prevent premature local buckling. The provided tables demonstrate how these limits are expressed as functions of the material parameter $\varepsilon$. For example, certain compression parts in the provided data are governed by limits such as: * **$h/t \leq 15\varepsilon$** and **$\frac{b+h}{2t} \leq 11,5\varepsilon$** * **$h/t \leq 11,9\varepsilon$** and **$\frac{b+h}{2t} \leq 9,1\varepsilon$** When performing a classification check, you must compare the actual width-to-thickness ratios of the I-section's flanges and web against the specific limits defined for the relevant stress distribution. If the section exceeds the limits for Class 1 or Class 2, it is classified into a lower class (Class 3 or 4), which restricts the design resistance to elastic analysis or requires effective section properties to account for local buckling. This follows EN 1993-1-4, Cl. 5.2.1 (Maximum width-to-thickness ratios) with supporting guidance from EN 1993-1-3, Cl. Table 5.1 (Maximum width-to-thickness ratios). --- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-4, Cl. 5.2.1 — Maximum width-to-thickness ratios

(1) The provisions for design by calculation given in this Part 1.4 may be assumed to apply to cross-sections within the dimensional limits given in EN 1993-1-3, except that the overall width-to-thickness ratios $b/t$ and $h/t$ as defined in EN 1993-1-3 should not exceed 400, see Figure 5.1.

(2) If visual distortion of flat elements of the cross-section are unacceptable under the serviceability loading, a limit of $b/t \leq 75$ may be applied.

response.answer:
The classification of I-sections under Eurocode 3 is determined by the local buckling resistance of their constituent compression elements, which are categorized into four distinct classes based on their ability to develop and maintain plastic resistance. According to clause 5.5.2, the four classes are defined by their structural performance: - **Class 1** cross-sections are those which can form a plastic hinge with the rotation capacity required from plastic analysis without reduction of the resistance. - **Class 2** cross-sections are those which can develop their plastic moment resistance. - **Class 3** and **Class 4** are determined by the limits specified in Table 5.2, as referenced in clause 5.2.2. Compression elements that do not meet the criteria for Class 3 are classified as Class 4 elements. The classification process relies on the slenderness of the compression elements, which is fundamentally influenced by the yield strength $f_y$. For the purpose of simplified rules, the parameter $\varepsilon$ is defined as **$\varepsilon = 0.85 \left[ \frac{235}{f_y} \right]^{0.5}$**, where $f_y$ is the yield strength at $20^{\circ}C$. It is essential to recognize that these classification boundaries are critical for determining the valid design method for a given structural component. For instance, in the context of a column web panel in shear, the design methods are only valid provided the column web slenderness satisfies the condition **$d_c/t_w \leq 69\varepsilon$**. When performing a desk review, ensure that the classification boundaries are checked in accordance with the specific requirements of the joint or member, as referenced in clause 5.2.2.5 and Figure 5.4. Note that these classification rules are distinct from joint rigidity checks, such as those for column bases or beam-to-column connections, where rigidity is determined by the initial stiffness $S_{j,ini}$ relative to the beam stiffness $EI_b / L_b$. This follows EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2 (Classification) with supporting guidance from EN 1993-1-4, Cl. 5.2.2 (Classification of compression elements). --- **References:**

response.answer:
Section classification for I-sections under EC3 is a method used to determine the local buckling resistance of the cross-section, categorizing it into one of four classes based on the width-to-thickness ratios of its compression elements. This classification determines whether the section can reach its plastic moment capacity or if local buckling limits its performance. The classification process is governed by the following principles defined in clause 5.5.2: * **Class 1:** Sections that can form a plastic hinge with the rotation capacity required for plastic analysis without reduction of resistance. * **Class 2:** Sections that can develop their plastic moment resistance but have limited rotation capacity due to local buckling. * **Class 3:** Sections where the extreme compression fibre can reach the yield strength assuming an elastic distribution of stresses, but local buckling prevents the development of the plastic moment resistance. * **Class 4:** Sections where local buckling occurs before the yield stress is reached in one or more parts of the cross-section. The classification depends on the width-to-thickness ratio of the parts subject to compression. Compression parts include every part of the cross-section that is either totally or partially in compression under the load combination considered. The cross-section is classified according to the highest (least favourable) class of its individual compression parts (e.g., the web or the flange). The limiting proportions for Class 1, 2, and 3 compression parts are obtained from Table 5.2. These limits are scaled by the parameter $\varepsilon$, which accounts for the steel yield strength $f_y$: $$\varepsilon = \sqrt{\frac{235}{f_y}}$$ Important considerations for classification include: * **Class 4 sections:** These may require the use of effective widths to account for reductions in resistance due to local buckling. * **Interaction:** Cross-sections with a Class 3 web and Class 1 or 2 flanges may be classified as Class 2 with an effective web. * **Shear:** Where the web is considered to resist shear forces only and is assumed not to contribute to bending or normal force resistance, the section may be designed based solely on the flange class. If a part fails to satisfy the limits for Class 3, it is classified as Class 4. This follows EN 1993-1-4, Cl. 5.2.1 (Maximum width-to-thickness ratios) with supporting guidance from EN 1993-1-3, Cl. Table 5.1 (Maximum width-to-thickness ratios). --- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-4, Cl. 5.2.1 — Maximum width-to-thickness ratios

(1) The provisions for design by calculation given in this Part 1.4 may be assumed to apply to cross-sections within the dimensional limits given in EN 1993-1-3, except that the overall width-to-thickness ratios $b/t$ and $h/t$ as defined in EN 1993-1-3 should not exceed 400, see Figure 5.1.

(2) If visual distortion of flat elements of the cross-section are unacceptable under the serviceability loading, a limit of $b/t \leq 75$ may be applied.

response.answer:
Cross-section classification defines the local buckling capacity of a member, while bolt shear resistance is determined by the bolt grade and the location of the shear plane. For an M20 Grade 8.8 bolt in single shear through the threaded portion, the design shear resistance is **$F_{v,Rd}$ = 94.08 kN** per bolt. ### Cross-Section Classification The classification of a cross-section is based on the width-to-thickness ratios of its compression parts. This classification determines the extent to which the section can resist loads before local buckling occurs. The four classes are defined as follows: * **Class 1:** Sections that can form a plastic hinge with the rotation capacity required for plastic analysis without resistance reduction. * **Class 2:** Sections that can develop their plastic moment resistance but have limited rotation capacity due to local buckling. * **Class 3:** Sections where the extreme compression fibre can reach yield strength assuming elastic stress distribution, but local buckling prevents the development of the plastic moment resistance. * **Class 4:** Sections where local buckling occurs before the attainment of yield stress in one or more parts. In these cases, effective widths are used to account for resistance reductions. **Key Classification Rules:** * A cross-section is classified according to the highest (least favourable) class of its individual compression parts (e.g., web or flange). * Compression parts include any part of the cross-section that is totally or partially in compression under the load combination considered. * Limiting proportions for Class 1, 2, and 3 parts are obtained from Table 5.2. Any part failing to meet Class 3 limits is classified as Class 4. ### Bolt Shear Resistance The design shear resistance of a bolt is calculated based on the bolt class, the tensile stress area, and the location of the shear plane relative to the threads. The general formula for shear resistance per shear plane is: $$F_{v,Rd} = \frac{\alpha_v f_{ub} A}{\gamma_{M2}}$$ Where: * $\alpha_v$ is a coefficient determined by the bolt class and shear plane location. For Grade 8.8 bolts, $\alpha_v = 0.6$. * $f_{ub}$ is the ultimate tensile strength of the bolt. * $A$ is the tensile stress area ($A_s$) if the shear plane passes through the threads, or the gross cross-section ($A_0$) if it passes through the unthreaded shank. * $\gamma_{M2}$ is the partial safety factor for bolt resistance. For the M20 Grade 8.8 bolt evaluated, with the shear plane passing through the threaded portion, the calculation is: $$F_{v,Rd} = \frac{0.6 \times 800 \text{ MPa} \times 245.0 \text{ mm}^2}{1.25} = 94.08 \text{ kN}$$ ### Important Caveats and Follow-up Checks While the shear resistance is a fundamental check, you must also verify the following to ensure the connection is adequate: * **Bearing Resistance ($F_{b,Rd}$):** This must be checked separately using the formula: $$F_{b,Rd} = \frac{k_1 \alpha_b f_u dt}{\gamma_{M2}}$$ Note that bearing resistance is sensitive to hole type; for example, oversized holes reduce the resistance to 0.8 times that of normal holes. * **Combined Shear and Tension:** If the bolt is subjected to both shear ($F_{v,Ed}$) and tension ($F_{t,Ed}$), you must verify the interaction: $$\frac{F_{v,Ed}}{F_{v,Rd}} + \frac{F_{t,Ed}}{1.4 F_{t,Rd}} \leq 1.0$$ * **Connection Category:** Depending on the design requirements (e.g., Category A, B, or C), you may need to check slip resistance or the net cross-section resistance at bolt holes. This follows EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2(1) (Classification of cross-sections) with supporting classification guidance from Cl. Table 5.2 (Width-to-thickness limits).

(1) Four classes of cross-sections are defined, as follows:

response.answer:
The classification of I-sections is determined by the width-to-thickness ratios of the individual compression elements (flanges and webs) to assess their susceptibility to local buckling, as defined in Clause 5.5.2. The classification process categorizes cross-sections into four distinct classes based on their ability to resist local buckling: * **Class 1**: Cross-sections that can form a plastic hinge with the rotation capacity required for plastic analysis without a reduction in resistance. * **Class 2**: Cross-sections that can develop their plastic moment resistance, but have limited rotation capacity due to local buckling. * **Class 3**: Cross-sections where the stress in the extreme compression fibre, assuming an elastic distribution, can reach the yield strength, but local buckling prevents the development of the plastic moment resistance. * **Class 4**: Cross-sections where local buckling occurs before the attainment of yield stress in one or more parts of the cross-section. ### Classification Methodology The classification of an I-section is governed by the following principles: * **Compression Parts**: Classification depends on the width-to-thickness ratio of every part of the cross-section that is either totally or partially in compression under the load combination considered (Clause 5.5.2(3) and (4)). * **Governing Class**: A cross-section is classified according to the highest (least favourable) class of its individual compression parts (Clause 5.5.2(6)). For example, if the web is **Class 3** and the flanges are **Class 1**, the entire section is classified as **Class 3**. * **Limiting Proportions**: The limiting proportions for **Class 1**, **Class 2**, and **Class 3** compression parts are determined using the parameter $\varepsilon$, defined as: $$\varepsilon = \sqrt{235/f_y}$$ A part that fails to satisfy the limits for **Class 3** is classified as **Class 4** (Clause 5.5.2(8)). ### Important Caveats and Exceptions When performing section classification, consider the following provisions: * **Effective Widths**: For **Class 4** sections, effective widths may be used to account for reductions in resistance due to local buckling (Clause 5.5.2(2)). * **Buckling Resistance**: When verifying the design buckling resistance of a member, the limiting proportions for **Class 3** must always be used (Clause 5.5.2(10)). * **Shear-Only Webs**: Where the web is considered to resist shear forces only and is assumed not to contribute to the bending and normal force resistance of the cross-section, the section may be designed as **Class 2**, **Class 3**, or **Class 4**, depending solely on the flange classification (Clause 5.5.2(12)). * **Alternative Classification**: Alternatively, the classification of a cross-section may be defined by quoting both the flange classification and the web classification separately (Clause 5.5.2(7)). This follows EN 1993-1-4, Cl. 5.2.1 (Maximum width-to-thickness ratios) with supporting guidance from EN 1993-1-3, Cl. Table 5.1 (Maximum width-to-thickness ratios). --- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-4, Cl. 5.2.1 — Maximum width-to-thickness ratios

(1) The provisions for design by calculation given in this Part 1.4 may be assumed to apply to cross-sections within the dimensional limits given in EN 1993-1-3, except that the overall width-to-thickness ratios $b/t$ and $h/t$ as defined in EN 1993-1-3 should not exceed 400, see Figure 5.1.

(2) If visual distortion of flat elements of the cross-section are unacceptable under the serviceability loading, a limit of $b/t \leq 75$ may be applied.

assumptions: Defaulted steel grade to S355 as none was specified.
Defaulted section name to IPE300 as none was specified.
Defaulted bolt parameters to standard values (8.8, 20mm, 1 shear plane, 1 bolt) as none were specified.

response.answer:
For an M20 Grade 8.8 bolt in single shear through the threaded portion, the design shear resistance is **$F_{v,Rd}$ = 94.08 kN** per bolt, while cross-section classification is governed by the width-to-thickness ratios of the individual compression elements, with the section assigned the highest (least favourable) class of its components. ### Cross-Section Classification (EC3) According to clause 5.5.2, cross-sections are classified into four categories based on their ability to resist local buckling and develop plastic or elastic resistance: * **Class 1:** Sections that can form a plastic hinge with the required rotation capacity for plastic analysis. * **Class 2:** Sections that can develop their plastic moment resistance but have limited rotation capacity. * **Class 3:** Sections where the extreme compression fibre can reach yield strength under elastic stress distribution, but local buckling prevents the development of plastic moment resistance. * **Class 4:** Sections where local buckling occurs before the attainment of yield stress in one or more parts. For these sections, effective widths must be used to account for resistance reductions. The classification of the entire cross-section is determined by the highest (least favourable) class of its individual compression parts (e.g., web or flange). While the classification generally depends on the width-to-thickness ratio of parts in compression, exceptions exist, such as when a web is considered to resist shear forces only, allowing the section to be designed based on the flange class. ### Bolt Shear Resistance The design shear resistance of a bolt is calculated based on the shear plane location and the bolt class. For a bolt in shear, the resistance per shear plane is determined using the following formula: $$F_{v,Rd} = \frac{\alpha_v f_{ub} A}{\gamma_{M2}}$$ Where: * $\alpha_v$ is the reduction factor (e.g., $0.6$ for 8.8 bolts where the shear plane passes through the threaded portion). * $f_{ub}$ is the ultimate tensile strength of the bolt. * $A$ is the tensile stress area ($A_s$) if the shear plane passes through the threads, or the gross cross-section ($A_0$) if it passes through the unthreaded portion. * $\gamma_{M2}$ is the partial safety factor. For an M20 Grade 8.8 bolt with the shear plane passing through the threaded portion, the calculation yields **$F_{v,Rd}$ = 94.08 kN**. **Important Caveats:** * **Bearing Resistance:** The shear resistance calculated above only covers the bolt itself. You must also verify the bearing resistance of the connected plates ($F_{b,Rd}$), which is calculated as: $$F_{b,Rd} = \frac{k_1 \alpha_b f_u dt}{\gamma_{M2}}$$ * **Combined Effects:** If the bolt is subjected to both shear and tension, you must verify the interaction using the criterion: $$\frac{F_{v,Ed}}{F_{v,Rd}} + \frac{F_{t,Ed}}{1.4 F_{t,Rd}} \leq 1.0$$ * **Hole Type:** Be aware that bearing resistance is reduced for oversized or slotted holes as specified in the notes of Table 3.4. This follows EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2(1) (Classification of cross-sections) with supporting classification guidance from Cl. Table 5.2 (Width-to-thickness limits).

--- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2(1) — Classification of cross-sections

(1) Four classes of cross-sections are defined, as follows:

- Class 1 cross-sections are those which can form a plastic hinge with the rotation capacity required from plastic analysis without reduction of the resistance.

- Class 2 cross-sections are those which can develop their plastic moment resistance, but have limited rotation capacity because of local buckling.

- Class 3 cross-sections are those in which the stress in the extreme compression fibre of the steel member assuming an elastic distribution of stresses can reach the yield strength, but local buckling is liable to prevent development of the plastic moment resistance.

- Class 4 cross-sections are those in which local buckling will occur before the attainment of yield stress in one or more parts of the cross-section.

(2) In Class 4 cross sections effective widths may be used to make the necessary allowances for reductions in resistance due to the effects of local buckling, see EN 1993-1-5, 4.4 AC2.

(3) The classification of a cross-section depends on the width to thickness ratio of the parts subject to compression.

(4) Compression parts include every part of a cross-section which is either totally or partially in compression under the load combination considered.

(5) The various compression parts in a cross-section (such as a web or flange) can, in general, be in different classes.

(6) A cross-section is classified according to the highest (least favourable) class of its compression parts. Exceptions are specified in 6.2.1(10) and 6.2.2.4(1).

(7) Alternatively the classification of a cross-section may be defined by quoting both the flange classification and the web classification.

(8) The limiting proportions for Class 1, 2, and 3 compression parts should be obtained from Table 5.2. A part which fails to satisfy the limits for Class 3 should be taken as Class 4.

(9) Except as given in (10) Class 4 sections may be treated as Class 3 sections if the width to thickness ratios are less than the limiting proportions for Class 3 obtained from Table 5.2 when $\varepsilon$ is increased by $\sqrt{\frac{f_y}{\gamma_{M0}}} \sigma_{com,Ed}$, where $\sigma_{com,Ed}$ is the maximum design compressive stress in the part taken from first order or where necessary second order analysis.

(10) However, when verifying the design buckling resistance of a member using section 6.3, the limiting proportions for Class 3 should always be obtained from Table 5.2.

(11) Cross-sections with a Class 3 web and Class 1 or 2 flanges may be classified as class 2 cross sections with an effective web in accordance with 6.2.2.4.

(12) Where the web is considered to resist shear forces only and is assumed not to contribute to the bending and normal force resistance of the cross section, the cross section may be designed as Class 2, 3 or 4 sections, depending only on the flange class.

NOTE For flange induced web buckling see EN 1993-1-5.

response.answer:
The classification of an I-section is determined by the width-to-thickness ratios of its individual compression parts, where the entire cross-section is assigned the classification of its most critical (highest) component. ### Classification Methodology According to Clause 5.5.2, the classification of a cross-section depends on the width-to-thickness ratio of the parts subject to compression. Compression parts include every part of a cross-section (such as the web or flange) that is either totally or partially in compression under the load combination considered. The classification system is defined as follows: * **Class 1:** Cross-sections that can form a plastic hinge with the rotation capacity required from plastic analysis without reduction of the resistance. * **Class 2:** Cross-sections that can develop their plastic moment resistance, but have limited rotation capacity because of local buckling. * **Class 3:** Cross-sections in which the stress in the extreme compression fibre, assuming an elastic distribution of stresses, can reach the yield strength, but local buckling is liable to prevent development of the plastic moment resistance. * **Class 4:** Cross-sections in which local buckling will occur before the attainment of yield stress in one or more parts of the cross-section. ### Determining the Class The classification is governed by the highest (least favourable) class of its compression parts. The limiting proportions for Class 1, 2, and 3 compression parts are obtained from Table 5.2. A part that fails to satisfy the limits for Class 3 is classified as Class 4. The limits are scaled by the factor $\varepsilon$, which accounts for the steel yield strength $f_y$: $$ \varepsilon = \sqrt{\frac{235}{f_y}} $$ ### Important Caveats and Exceptions * **Class 4 Sections:** In Class 4 cross-sections, effective widths may be used to make the necessary allowances for reductions in resistance due to the effects of local buckling. * **Web and Flange Interaction:** Cross-sections with a Class 3 web and Class 1 or 2 flanges may be classified as Class 2 cross-sections with an effective web. * **Shear-Only Webs:** Where the web is considered to resist shear forces only and is assumed not to contribute to the bending and normal force resistance of the cross-section, the cross-section may be designed as Class 2, 3, or 4, depending only on the flange class. * **Alternative Classification:** Alternatively, the classification of a cross-section may be defined by quoting both the flange classification and the web classification. This follows EN 1993-1-4, Cl. 5.2.1 (Maximum width-to-thickness ratios) with supporting guidance from EN 1993-1-3, Cl. Table 5.1 (Maximum width-to-thickness ratios). --- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-4, Cl. 5.2.1 — Maximum width-to-thickness ratios

(1) The provisions for design by calculation given in this Part 1.4 may be assumed to apply to cross-sections within the dimensional limits given in EN 1993-1-3, except that the overall width-to-thickness ratios $b/t$ and $h/t$ as defined in EN 1993-1-3 should not exceed 400, see Figure 5.1.

(2) If visual distortion of flat elements of the cross-section are unacceptable under the serviceability loading, a limit of $b/t \leq 75$ may be applied.

response.answer:
For the IPE300 section in S355 steel, the cross-section is classified as Class 4, resulting in a design bending resistance of **$M_{Rd} = 197.74$ kNm**, a design axial resistance of **$N_{Rd} = 1909.9$ kN**, and a design shear resistance of **$V_{Rd} = 563.64$ kN**. ### Cross-Section Classification According to clause 5.5.2, cross-sections are categorized into four classes based on the width-to-thickness ratios of their compression parts. This classification determines the extent to which the section can develop its resistance: * **Class 1 & 2:** Capable of developing plastic moment resistance. * **Class 3:** Capable of reaching yield strength in the extreme compression fiber, but local buckling prevents plastic moment development. * **Class 4:** Local buckling occurs before yield stress is attained. For these sections, effective widths must be used to account for reductions in resistance (clause 5.5.2(2)). The classification of the entire section is governed by the highest (least favorable) class of its individual compression parts (clause 5.5.2(6)). In this instance, the IPE300 is classified as Class 4. ### Shear Resistance The design shear resistance is calculated as **$V_{Rd} = 563.64$ kN** using the following formula: $$V_{Rd} = \frac{A_v f_y}{\sqrt{3} \gamma_{M0}}$$ Where $A_v$ is the shear area, $f_y$ is the yield strength, and $\gamma_{M0}$ is the partial safety factor. ### Bending Resistance and Interaction The bending resistance is **$M_{Rd} = 197.74$ kNm**, calculated using the section modulus $W$ and yield strength $f_y$. When verifying bending resistance, you must consider the interaction with shear force (clause 6.2.8): * If the design shear force $V_{Ed} \leq 0.5 V_{pl,Rd}$, the effect of shear on bending resistance may be neglected. * If $V_{Ed} > 0.5 V_{pl,Rd}$, the bending resistance must be calculated using a reduced yield strength $(1 - \rho) f_y$ for the shear area, where the reduction factor $\rho$ is defined as: $$\rho = \left( \frac{2 V_{Ed}}{V_{pl,Rd}} - 1 \right)^2$$ For Class 4 sections, ensure that all verifications utilize effective cross-sectional properties to account for local buckling effects as required by clause 6.2.1(2). Note that member buckling and lateral torsional buckling checks are separate from these cross-sectional resistance verifications. This follows EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2(1) (Classification of cross-sections) with supporting classification guidance from Cl. Table 5.2 (Width-to-thickness limits).

Assumptions used - Default steel grade S355 assumed. - Default section IPE300 assumed. - Default partial safety factor γM0 = 1.0 assumed.
--- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2(1) — Classification of cross-sections

(1) Four classes of cross-sections are defined, as follows:

- Class 1 cross-sections are those which can form a plastic hinge with the rotation capacity required from plastic analysis without reduction of the resistance.

- Class 2 cross-sections are those which can develop their plastic moment resistance, but have limited rotation capacity because of local buckling.

- Class 3 cross-sections are those in which the stress in the extreme compression fibre of the steel member assuming an elastic distribution of stresses can reach the yield strength, but local buckling is liable to prevent development of the plastic moment resistance.

- Class 4 cross-sections are those in which local buckling will occur before the attainment of yield stress in one or more parts of the cross-section.

(2) In Class 4 cross sections effective widths may be used to make the necessary allowances for reductions in resistance due to the effects of local buckling, see EN 1993-1-5, 4.4 AC2.

(3) The classification of a cross-section depends on the width to thickness ratio of the parts subject to compression.

(4) Compression parts include every part of a cross-section which is either totally or partially in compression under the load combination considered.

(5) The various compression parts in a cross-section (such as a web or flange) can, in general, be in different classes.

(6) A cross-section is classified according to the highest (least favourable) class of its compression parts. Exceptions are specified in 6.2.1(10) and 6.2.2.4(1).

(7) Alternatively the classification of a cross-section may be defined by quoting both the flange classification and the web classification.

(8) The limiting proportions for Class 1, 2, and 3 compression parts should be obtained from Table 5.2. A part which fails to satisfy the limits for Class 3 should be taken as Class 4.

(9) Except as given in (10) Class 4 sections may be treated as Class 3 sections if the width to thickness ratios are less than the limiting proportions for Class 3 obtained from Table 5.2 when $\varepsilon$ is increased by $\sqrt{\frac{f_y}{\gamma_{M0}}} \sigma_{com,Ed}$, where $\sigma_{com,Ed}$ is the maximum design compressive stress in the part taken from first order or where necessary second order analysis.

(10) However, when verifying the design buckling resistance of a member using section 6.3, the limiting proportions for Class 3 should always be obtained from Table 5.2.

(11) Cross-sections with a Class 3 web and Class 1 or 2 flanges may be classified as class 2 cross sections with an effective web in accordance with 6.2.2.4.

(12) Where the web is considered to resist shear forces only and is assumed not to contribute to the bending and normal force resistance of the cross section, the cross section may be designed as Class 2, 3 or 4 sections, depending only on the flange class.

NOTE For flange induced web buckling see EN 1993-1-5.

2. EN 1993-1-1, Table 5.2 — Width-to-thickness limits

Angles

Refer also to “Outstand flanges” (see sheet 2 of 3)

Does not apply to angles in continuous contact with other components

Class

Section in compression

Stress distribution across section (compression positive)

$$h/t \leq 15\varepsilon$$ and $$\frac{b+h}{2t} \leq 11,5\varepsilon$$

Tubular sections

Class

Section in bending and/or compression

$$d/t \leq 50\varepsilon^2$$

$$d/t \leq 70\varepsilon^2$$

$$d/t \leq 90\varepsilon^2$$

NOTE For $d/t > 90\varepsilon^2$ see EN 1993-1-6.

$$\varepsilon = \sqrt{235/f_y}$$

$$f_y$$ 235 275 355 420 460

$$\varepsilon$$ 1,00 0,92 0,81 0,75 0,71

$$\varepsilon^2$$ 1,00 0,85 0,66 0,56 0,51

3. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.5(1) — Bending resistance

(1)P The design value of the bending moment $M_{Ed}$ at each cross-section shall satisfy:

$$\frac{M_{Ed}}{M_{c,Rd}} \leq 1,0$$

where $M_{c,Rd}$ is determined considering fastener holes, see (4) to (6).

(2) The design resistance for bending about one principal axis of a cross-section is determined as follows:

$$M_{c,Rd} = M_{pl,Rd} = \frac{W_{pl} f_y}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 1 or 2 cross sections

$$M_{c,Rd} = M_{el,Rd} = \frac{W_{el,\min} f_y}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 3 cross sections

$$M_{c,Rd} = \frac{W_{eff,\min} f_y}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 4 cross sections

where $W_{el,\min}$ and $W_{eff,\min}$ corresponds to the fibre with the maximum elastic stress.

(3) For bending about both axes, the methods given in 6.2.9 should be used.

(4) Fastener holes in the tension flange may be ignored provided that for the tension flange:

$$\frac{A_{f,net} 0,9 f_u}{\gamma_{M2}} \geq \frac{A_f f_y}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

where $A_f$ is the area of the tension flange.

NOTE The criterion in (4) provides capacity design (see 1.5.8).

(5) Fastener holes in tension zone of the web need not be allowed for, provided that the limit given in (4) is satisfied for the complete tension zone comprising the tension flange plus the tension zone of the web.

(6) Fastener holes except for oversize and slotted holes in compression zone of the cross-section need not be allowed for, provided that they are filled by fasteners.

4. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.4(1) — Compression resistance

(1)P The design value of the compression force $N_{Ed}$ at each cross-section shall satisfy:

$$\frac{N_{Ed}}{N_{c,Rd}} \leq 1,0$$

(2) The design resistance of the cross-section for uniform compression $N_{c,Rd}$ should be determined as follows:

$$N_{c,Rd} = \frac{A f_y}{\gamma_{M0}} \text{ for class 1, 2 or 3 cross-sections}$$

$$N_{c,Rd} = \frac{A_{eff} f_y}{\gamma_{M0}} \text{ for class 4 cross-sections}$$

(3) Fastener holes except for oversize and slotted holes as defined in EN 1090 need not be allowed for in compression members, provided that they are filled by fasteners.

(4) In the case of unsymmetrical Class 4 sections, the method given in 6.2.9.3 should be used to allow for the additional moment $\Delta M_{Ed}$ due to the eccentricity of the centroidal axis of the effective section, see 6.2.2.5(4).

5. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.6(1) — Shear resistance

(1)P The design value of the shear force $V_{Ed}$ at each cross section shall satisfy:

$$\frac{V_{Ed}}{V_{c,Rd}} \leq 1,0$$

where $V_{c,Rd}$ is the design shear resistance. For plastic design $V_{c,Rd}$ is the design plastic shear resistance $V_{pl,Rd}$ as given in (2). For elastic design $V_{c,Rd}$ is the design elastic shear resistance calculated using (4) and (5).

(2) In the absence of torsion the design plastic shear resistance is given by:

$$V_{pl,Rd} = \frac{A_v \left( f_y / \sqrt{3} \right)}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

where $A_v$ is the shear area.

(3) The shear area $A_v$ may be taken as follows:

a) rolled I and H sections, load parallel to web
$$A - 2bt_f + (t_w + 2r)t_f$$ but not less than $\eta h_w t_w$

b) rolled channel sections, load parallel to web
$$A - 2bt_f + (t_w + r)t_f$$

c) rolled T-section, load parallel to web

- for rolled T-sections:
$$A_v = A - bt_f + (t_w + 2r)\frac{t_f}{2}$$

- for welded T-sections:
$$A_v = t_w(h - \frac{t_f}{2})$$

d) welded I, H and box sections, load parallel to web
$$\eta \sum (h_w t_w)$$

e) welded I, H, channel and box sections, load parallel to flanges
$$A - \sum (h_w t_w)$$

f) rolled rectangular hollow sections of uniform thickness:

- load parallel to depth
Ah/(b+h)

- load parallel to width
Ab/(b+h)

g) circular hollow sections and tubes of uniform thickness
$$2A/\pi$$

where $A$ is the crosssectional area;

b is the overall breadth;

h is the overall depth;

$h_w$ is the depth of the web;

r is the root radius;

$t_f$ is the flange thickness;

$t_w$ is the web thickness (If the web thickness in not constant, $t_w$ should be taken as the minimum thickness.)

$\eta$ see EN 1993-1-5.

NOTE $\eta$ may be conservatively taken equal 1,0.

(4) For verifying the design elastic shear resistance $V_{c,Rd}$ the following criterion for a critical point of the cross section may be used unless the buckling verification in section 5 of EN 1993-1-5 applies:

$$\frac{\tau_{Ed}}{f_y/\left(\sqrt{3}\gamma_{M0}\right)} \leq 1.0$$

where $\tau_{Ed}$ may be obtained from:
$$\tau_{Ed} = \frac{V_{Ed} S}{I t}$$

where $V_{Ed}$ is the design value of the shear force

S is the first moment of area about the centroidal axis of that portion of the cross-section between the point at which the shear is required and the boundary of the cross-section

I is second moment of area of the whole cross section

t is the thickness at the examined point

NOTE The verification according to (4) is conservative as it excludes partial plastic shear distribution, which is permitted in elastic design, see (5). Therefore it should only be carried out where the verification on the basis of $V_{c,Rd}$ according to equation (6.17) cannot be performed.

BS EN 1993-1-1:2005
EN 1993-1-1:2005 (E)

(5) For I- or H-sections the shear stress in the web may be taken as:

$$\tau_{\text{Ed}} = \frac{V_{\text{Ed}}}{A_w} \text{ if } A_f / A_w \geq 0, 6$$

(6.21)

where $A_f$ is the area of one flange;
$A_w$ is the area of the web: $A_w = h_w t_w$.

response.answer:
This follows EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2 (Classification) with supporting guidance from EN 1993-1-4, Cl. 5.2.2 (Classification of compression elements). --- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2 — Classification

(1) Four classes of cross-sections are defined, as follows:

- Class 1 cross-sections are those which can form a plastic hinge with the rotation capacity required from plastic analysis without reduction of the resistance.

- Class 2 cross-sections are those which can develop their plastic moment resistance, but have limited rotation capacity because of local buckling.

- Class 3 cross-sections are those in which the stress in the extreme compression fibre of the steel member assuming an elastic distribution of stresses can reach the yield strength, but local buckling is liable to prevent development of the plastic moment resistance.

- Class 4 cross-sections are those in which local buckling will occur before the attainment of yield stress in one or more parts of the cross-section.

(2) In Class 4 cross sections effective widths may be used to make the necessary allowances for reductions in resistance due to the effects of local buckling, see EN 1993-1-5, 4.4 AC2.

(3) The classification of a cross-section depends on the width to thickness ratio of the parts subject to compression.

(4) Compression parts include every part of a cross-section which is either totally or partially in compression under the load combination considered.

(5) The various compression parts in a cross-section (such as a web or flange) can, in general, be in different classes.

(6) A cross-section is classified according to the highest (least favourable) class of its compression parts. Exceptions are specified in 6.2.1(10) and 6.2.2.4(1).

(7) Alternatively the classification of a cross-section may be defined by quoting both the flange classification and the web classification.

(8) The limiting proportions for Class 1, 2, and 3 compression parts should be obtained from Table 5.2. A part which fails to satisfy the limits for Class 3 should be taken as Class 4.

(9) Except as given in (10) Class 4 sections may be treated as Class 3 sections if the width to thickness ratios are less than the limiting proportions for Class 3 obtained from Table 5.2 when $\varepsilon$ is increased by $\sqrt{\frac{f_y}{\gamma_{M0}}} \sigma_{com,Ed}$, where $\sigma_{com,Ed}$ is the maximum design compressive stress in the part taken from first order or where necessary second order analysis.

(10) However, when verifying the design buckling resistance of a member using section 6.3, the limiting proportions for Class 3 should always be obtained from Table 5.2.

(11) Cross-sections with a Class 3 web and Class 1 or 2 flanges may be classified as class 2 cross sections with an effective web in accordance with 6.2.2.4.

(12) Where the web is considered to resist shear forces only and is assumed not to contribute to the bending and normal force resistance of the cross section, the cross section may be designed as Class 2, 3 or 4 sections, depending only on the flange class.

NOTE For flange induced web buckling see EN 1993-1-5.

2. EN 1993-1-4, Cl. 5.2.2 — Classification of compression elements

(1) Compression elements of cross-sections should be classified as Class 1, 2 or 3 depending upon the limits specified in Table 5.2. Those compression elements that do not meet the criteria for Class 3 should be classified as Class 4 elements.

EN 1993-1-4: 2006 (E)

request.message: Calculate bolt shear resistance for 4x M20 grade 8.8 bolts, single shear plane

meta.rationale: Pure calculation intent detected; retrieval skipped in thinking mode.

assumptions: Assumed shear plane passes through threads (conservative default).
Assumed partial safety factor gamma_M2 = 1.25 (standard EC3 value).

response.answer:
For a connection utilizing 4x M20 Grade 8.8 bolts in single shear through the threaded portion, the total design shear resistance is **$F_{v,Rd,total}$ = 376.32 kN**, with each individual bolt providing a design shear resistance of **$F_{v,Rd}$ = 94.08 kN**. The shear resistance is calculated based on the capacity of the bolt threads, which is the standard conservative assumption when the shear plane intersects the threaded portion. The design shear resistance per shear plane is determined using the following formula: $$F_{v,Rd} = \frac{\alpha_v \cdot f_{ub} \cdot A}{\gamma_{M2}}$$ Substituting the parameters for an M20 Grade 8.8 bolt: * $\alpha_v = 0.6$ (shear reduction factor) * $f_{ub} = 800$ N/mm$^2$ (ultimate tensile strength of the bolt) * $A = 245.0$ mm$^2$ (tensile stress area of an M20 bolt) * $\gamma_{M2} = 1.25$ (partial safety factor) The calculation is as follows: $$F_{v,Rd} = \frac{0.6 \times 800 \times 245.0}{1.25} = 94.08 \text{ kN}$$ It is important to note that this calculation assumes the shear plane intersects the threaded portion of the bolt. To complete the connection design, please ensure the following additional checks are performed: * **Bearing Resistance:** You must verify the bearing resistance of the connected plates, as this often governs the connection capacity rather than the bolt shear itself. * **Force Distribution:** Ensure the design force is distributed appropriately among the 4 bolts, accounting for any potential eccentricities or group effects in the connection geometry. This is checked against EN 1993-1-8, Cl. Table 3.4 (Design resistance for bolts in shear).

Bearing resistance 1), 2), 3)

$$F_{b,Rd} = \frac{k_1 \alpha_b f_u dt}{\gamma_M 2}$$

where $\alpha_b$ is the smallest of $\alpha_d$; $\frac{f_{ub}}{f_u}$ or 1,0;

in the direction of load transfer:

- for end bolts: $\alpha_d = \frac{e_1}{3d_0}$; for inner bolts: $\alpha_d = \frac{p_1}{3d_0} - \frac{1}{4}$ perpendicular to the direction of load transfer:

$$F_{ac2} -$$ for edge bolts: $k_1$ is the smallest of $2,8 \frac{e_2}{d_0} - 1,7,1,4 \frac{p_2}{d_0} - 1,7$ and $2,5 \frac{p_2}{d_0} - 1,7$ or 2,5

- for inner bolts: $k_1$ is the smallest of $1,4 \frac{p_2}{d_0} - 1,7$ or 2,5

Tension resistance 2)

$$F_{t,Rd} = \frac{k_2 f_{ub} A_s}{\gamma_M 2}$$

where $k_2 = 0,63$ for countersunk bolt, otherwise $k_2 = 0,9$.

1) The bearing resistance $F_{b,Rd}$ for bolts

- in oversized holes is 0,8 times the bearing resistance for bolts in normal holes.

- in slotted holes, where the longitudinal axis of the slotted hole is perpendicular to the direction of the force transfer, is 0,6 times the bearing resistance for bolts in round, normal holes.

2) For countersunk bolt:

- the bearing resistance $F_{b,Rd}$ should be based on a plate thickness $t$ equal to the thickness of the connected plate minus half the depth of the countersinking.

- for the determination of the tension resistance $F_{t,Rd}$ the angle and depth of countersinking should conform with 1.2.4 Reference Standards: Group 4, otherwise the tension resistance $F_{t,Rd}$ should be adjusted accordingly.

3) When the load on a bolt is not parallel to the edge, the bearing resistance may be verified separately for the bolt load components parallel and normal to the end.

The classification of an I-section is determined by the width-to-thickness ratios of its individual compression elements, with the overall section classified according to the least favourable (highest) class of its constituent parts. ### Definition of Cross-Section Classes The classification system defines the ability of a cross-section to resist loads based on its susceptibility to local buckling: * **Class 1**: These sections can form a plastic hinge with the rotation capacity required for plastic analysis without a reduction in resistance. * **Class 2**: These sections can develop their plastic moment resistance, but possess limited rotation capacity due to local buckling. * **Class 3**: In these sections, the stress in the extreme compression fibre can reach the yield strength assuming an elastic distribution of stresses, but local buckling prevents the development of the plastic moment resistance. * **Class 4**: Local buckling occurs before the yield stress is attained in one or more parts of the cross-section. ### Classification Procedure The classification depends on the width-to-thickness ratio of the parts subject to compression. Compression parts are defined as any part of the cross-section that is either totally or partially in compression under the load combination being considered. * The limiting proportions for **Class 1**, **Class 2**, and **Class 3** compression parts are obtained from Table 5.2. * Any part that fails to satisfy the limits for **Class 3** is automatically classified as **Class 4**. * For **Class 4** cross-sections, effective widths may be used to account for reductions in resistance due to local buckling, in accordance with EN 1993-1-5. * While a section is generally classified by the highest class of its compression parts, you may alternatively define the classification by quoting the flange and web classifications separately. ### Important Exceptions and Nuances There are specific scenarios where the standard classification rules are modified: * **Class 3 Web with Class 1 or 2 Flanges**: These may be classified as **Class 2** cross-sections, provided an effective web is used in accordance with 6.2.2.4. * **Shear-Resisting Webs**: Where the web is considered to resist shear forces only and is assumed not to contribute to the bending and normal force resistance, the cross-section may be designed as **Class 2**, **Class 3**, or **Class 4**, depending solely on the flange class. * **Stress-Based Classification for Class 4**: Except when verifying design buckling resistance (which must always use Table 5.2 limits), **Class 4** sections may be treated as **Class 3** if the width-to-thickness ratios are less than the limits for **Class 3** when $\varepsilon$ is increased by $\sqrt{\frac{f_y}{\gamma_{M0}}} \sigma_{com,Ed}$, where $\sigma_{com,Ed}$ is the maximum design compressive stress in the part. This follows EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2 (Classification) with supporting guidance from EN 1993-1-4, Cl. 5.2.2 (Classification of compression elements). --- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2 — Classification

(1) Four classes of cross-sections are defined, as follows:

- Class 1 cross-sections are those which can form a plastic hinge with the rotation capacity required from plastic analysis without reduction of the resistance.

- Class 2 cross-sections are those which can develop their plastic moment resistance, but have limited rotation capacity because of local buckling.

- Class 3 cross-sections are those in which the stress in the extreme compression fibre of the steel member assuming an elastic distribution of stresses can reach the yield strength, but local buckling is liable to prevent development of the plastic moment resistance.

- Class 4 cross-sections are those in which local buckling will occur before the attainment of yield stress in one or more parts of the cross-section.

(2) In Class 4 cross sections effective widths may be used to make the necessary allowances for reductions in resistance due to the effects of local buckling, see EN 1993-1-5, 4.4 AC2.

(3) The classification of a cross-section depends on the width to thickness ratio of the parts subject to compression.

(4) Compression parts include every part of a cross-section which is either totally or partially in compression under the load combination considered.

(5) The various compression parts in a cross-section (such as a web or flange) can, in general, be in different classes.

(6) A cross-section is classified according to the highest (least favourable) class of its compression parts. Exceptions are specified in 6.2.1(10) and 6.2.2.4(1).

(7) Alternatively the classification of a cross-section may be defined by quoting both the flange classification and the web classification.

(8) The limiting proportions for Class 1, 2, and 3 compression parts should be obtained from Table 5.2. A part which fails to satisfy the limits for Class 3 should be taken as Class 4.

(9) Except as given in (10) Class 4 sections may be treated as Class 3 sections if the width to thickness ratios are less than the limiting proportions for Class 3 obtained from Table 5.2 when $\varepsilon$ is increased by $\sqrt{\frac{f_y}{\gamma_{M0}}} \sigma_{com,Ed}$, where $\sigma_{com,Ed}$ is the maximum design compressive stress in the part taken from first order or where necessary second order analysis.

(10) However, when verifying the design buckling resistance of a member using section 6.3, the limiting proportions for Class 3 should always be obtained from Table 5.2.

(11) Cross-sections with a Class 3 web and Class 1 or 2 flanges may be classified as class 2 cross sections with an effective web in accordance with 6.2.2.4.

(12) Where the web is considered to resist shear forces only and is assumed not to contribute to the bending and normal force resistance of the cross section, the cross section may be designed as Class 2, 3 or 4 sections, depending only on the flange class.

NOTE For flange induced web buckling see EN 1993-1-5.

2. EN 1993-1-4, Cl. 5.2.2 — Classification of compression elements

(1) Compression elements of cross-sections should be classified as Class 1, 2 or 3 depending upon the limits specified in Table 5.2. Those compression elements that do not meet the criteria for Class 3 should be classified as Class 4 elements.

EN 1993-1-4: 2006 (E)

response.answer:
The classification of an I-section under EN 1993-1-1 is determined by the width-to-thickness ratios of its individual compression elements (flanges and web), where the overall section class is governed by the **highest (least favourable) class** of any of its constituent parts. The classification system defines the ability of the cross-section to resist loads based on its susceptibility to local buckling, as outlined in Clause 5.5.2: * **Class 1:** Cross-sections that can form a plastic hinge with the rotation capacity required for plastic analysis without reduction of resistance. * **Class 2:** Cross-sections that can develop their plastic moment resistance, but have limited rotation capacity due to local buckling. * **Class 3:** Cross-sections where the extreme compression fibre can reach the yield strength assuming an elastic stress distribution, but local buckling prevents the development of the plastic moment resistance. * **Class 4:** Cross-sections where local buckling occurs before the yield stress is attained in one or more parts of the cross-section. ### Classification Logic and Parameters The classification process relies on the following principles: * **Compression Parts:** Any part of the cross-section that is either totally or partially in compression under the load combination considered must be classified (Clause 5.5.2(4)). * **Governing Class:** A cross-section is classified according to the highest (least favourable) class of its compression parts (Clause 5.5.2(6)). Alternatively, you may define the section by explicitly quoting both the flange and web classifications (Clause 5.5.2(7)). * **Limiting Proportions:** The limiting width-to-thickness ratios for Class 1, 2, and 3 parts are obtained from Table 5.2. Any part failing to satisfy the Class 3 limits is automatically classified as Class 4 (Clause 5.5.2(8)). ### Important Exceptions and Caveats There are specific scenarios where the standard classification rules are modified: * **Class 4 Treatment:** While Class 4 sections generally require the use of effective widths to account for local buckling (Clause 5.5.2(2)), they may be treated as Class 3 sections in certain stress conditions (Clause 5.5.2(9)). However, when verifying design buckling resistance using section 6.3, the limits for Class 3 must always be obtained from Table 5.2 (Clause 5.5.2(10)). * **Class 3 Web / Class 1 or 2 Flanges:** Cross-sections with a Class 3 web and Class 1 or 2 flanges may be classified as **Class 2** cross-sections, provided an effective web is used in accordance with 6.2.2.4 (Clause 5.5.2(11)). * **Shear-Only Webs:** If the web is considered to resist shear forces only and is assumed not to contribute to the bending and normal force resistance of the cross-section, the section may be designed as Class 2, 3, or 4, depending solely on the flange classification (Clause 5.5.2(12)). This follows EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2 (Classification) with supporting guidance from EN 1993-1-4, Cl. 5.2.2 (Classification of compression elements). --- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2 — Classification

(1) Four classes of cross-sections are defined, as follows:

- Class 1 cross-sections are those which can form a plastic hinge with the rotation capacity required from plastic analysis without reduction of the resistance.

- Class 2 cross-sections are those which can develop their plastic moment resistance, but have limited rotation capacity because of local buckling.

- Class 3 cross-sections are those in which the stress in the extreme compression fibre of the steel member assuming an elastic distribution of stresses can reach the yield strength, but local buckling is liable to prevent development of the plastic moment resistance.

- Class 4 cross-sections are those in which local buckling will occur before the attainment of yield stress in one or more parts of the cross-section.

(2) In Class 4 cross sections effective widths may be used to make the necessary allowances for reductions in resistance due to the effects of local buckling, see EN 1993-1-5, 4.4 AC2.

(3) The classification of a cross-section depends on the width to thickness ratio of the parts subject to compression.

(4) Compression parts include every part of a cross-section which is either totally or partially in compression under the load combination considered.

(5) The various compression parts in a cross-section (such as a web or flange) can, in general, be in different classes.

(6) A cross-section is classified according to the highest (least favourable) class of its compression parts. Exceptions are specified in 6.2.1(10) and 6.2.2.4(1).

(7) Alternatively the classification of a cross-section may be defined by quoting both the flange classification and the web classification.

(8) The limiting proportions for Class 1, 2, and 3 compression parts should be obtained from Table 5.2. A part which fails to satisfy the limits for Class 3 should be taken as Class 4.

(9) Except as given in (10) Class 4 sections may be treated as Class 3 sections if the width to thickness ratios are less than the limiting proportions for Class 3 obtained from Table 5.2 when $\varepsilon$ is increased by $\sqrt{\frac{f_y}{\gamma_{M0}}} \sigma_{com,Ed}$, where $\sigma_{com,Ed}$ is the maximum design compressive stress in the part taken from first order or where necessary second order analysis.

(10) However, when verifying the design buckling resistance of a member using section 6.3, the limiting proportions for Class 3 should always be obtained from Table 5.2.

(11) Cross-sections with a Class 3 web and Class 1 or 2 flanges may be classified as class 2 cross sections with an effective web in accordance with 6.2.2.4.

(12) Where the web is considered to resist shear forces only and is assumed not to contribute to the bending and normal force resistance of the cross section, the cross section may be designed as Class 2, 3 or 4 sections, depending only on the flange class.

NOTE For flange induced web buckling see EN 1993-1-5.

2. EN 1993-1-4, Cl. 5.2.2 — Classification of compression elements

(1) Compression elements of cross-sections should be classified as Class 1, 2 or 3 depending upon the limits specified in Table 5.2. Those compression elements that do not meet the criteria for Class 3 should be classified as Class 4 elements.

EN 1993-1-4: 2006 (E)

request.message: Given IPE300, S355, what is the bending resistance? Assume typical parameters if missing.

meta.rationale: Pure calculation intent detected; retrieval skipped in thinking mode.

assumptions: Assumed gamma_M0 = 1.0 as per standard Eurocode 3 design practice.

response.answer:
For an IPE300 section in S355 steel, the design bending resistance is **$M_{c,Rd} = 197.74$ kNm**, calculated using a section modulus of $W = 557.0$ cm$^3$ and a partial safety factor of $\gamma_{M0} = 1.0$. The calculation is based on the standard resistance formula for a cross-section: $$M_{Rd} = \frac{W \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{M0}}$$ Where the parameters are defined as follows: * $W = 557.0$ cm$^3$: The section modulus used for the calculation. * $f_y = 355$ MPa: The yield strength of the S355 steel. * $\gamma_{M0} = 1.0$: The partial safety factor for cross-section resistance. It is important to note the following engineering considerations regarding this result: * **Section Classification:** The IPE300 section is classified as Class 4. This indicates that local buckling of the web may occur before the full plastic moment capacity is reached. The provided resistance assumes that effective section properties have been accounted for in the $W$ value used. * **Lateral-Torsional Buckling (LTB):** This value represents the cross-sectional resistance only. It assumes the member is fully restrained against lateral-torsional buckling. If the beam is not fully restrained, a separate LTB check is required, which will likely reduce the design capacity significantly. * **Global Buckling:** This calculation does not account for global member buckling or interaction with axial forces. If the member is subject to combined bending and axial compression, an interaction check must be performed. This follows EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2(1) (Classification of cross-sections) with supporting classification guidance from Cl. Table 5.2 (Width-to-thickness limits).

--- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2(1) — Classification of cross-sections

(1) Four classes of cross-sections are defined, as follows:

- Class 1 cross-sections are those which can form a plastic hinge with the rotation capacity required from plastic analysis without reduction of the resistance.

- Class 2 cross-sections are those which can develop their plastic moment resistance, but have limited rotation capacity because of local buckling.

- Class 3 cross-sections are those in which the stress in the extreme compression fibre of the steel member assuming an elastic distribution of stresses can reach the yield strength, but local buckling is liable to prevent development of the plastic moment resistance.

- Class 4 cross-sections are those in which local buckling will occur before the attainment of yield stress in one or more parts of the cross-section.

(2) In Class 4 cross sections effective widths may be used to make the necessary allowances for reductions in resistance due to the effects of local buckling, see EN 1993-1-5, 4.4 AC2.

(3) The classification of a cross-section depends on the width to thickness ratio of the parts subject to compression.

(4) Compression parts include every part of a cross-section which is either totally or partially in compression under the load combination considered.

(5) The various compression parts in a cross-section (such as a web or flange) can, in general, be in different classes.

(6) A cross-section is classified according to the highest (least favourable) class of its compression parts. Exceptions are specified in 6.2.1(10) and 6.2.2.4(1).

(7) Alternatively the classification of a cross-section may be defined by quoting both the flange classification and the web classification.

(8) The limiting proportions for Class 1, 2, and 3 compression parts should be obtained from Table 5.2. A part which fails to satisfy the limits for Class 3 should be taken as Class 4.

(9) Except as given in (10) Class 4 sections may be treated as Class 3 sections if the width to thickness ratios are less than the limiting proportions for Class 3 obtained from Table 5.2 when $\varepsilon$ is increased by $\sqrt{\frac{f_y}{\gamma_{M0}}} \sigma_{com,Ed}$, where $\sigma_{com,Ed}$ is the maximum design compressive stress in the part taken from first order or where necessary second order analysis.

(10) However, when verifying the design buckling resistance of a member using section 6.3, the limiting proportions for Class 3 should always be obtained from Table 5.2.

(11) Cross-sections with a Class 3 web and Class 1 or 2 flanges may be classified as class 2 cross sections with an effective web in accordance with 6.2.2.4.

(12) Where the web is considered to resist shear forces only and is assumed not to contribute to the bending and normal force resistance of the cross section, the cross section may be designed as Class 2, 3 or 4 sections, depending only on the flange class.

NOTE For flange induced web buckling see EN 1993-1-5.

2. EN 1993-1-1, Table 5.2 — Width-to-thickness limits

Angles

Refer also to “Outstand flanges” (see sheet 2 of 3)

Does not apply to angles in continuous contact with other components

$$h/t \leq 15\varepsilon$$ and $$\frac{b+h}{2t} \leq 11,5\varepsilon$$
